jokes.text: Hey, Jack, the vending machine is broken! / I know. I broke it. I needed to speak with you, and I knew that was the fastest way to get you up here.
jokes.setup: Liz reports a broken vending machine as if it's a problem
jokes.punchline: Jack reveals he deliberately broke it to manipulate Liz into coming upstairs
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Jack's corporate manipulation meets Liz's food obsession. Clean setup/payoff with great economy.

jokes.text: You bastard! I trusted you!
jokes.setup: Jack's admission of vending machine sabotage
jokes.punchline: Liz's melodramatic betrayal response over snack access
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Liz's food priorities through mock-dramatic language.

jokes.text: I'm doing God's work here in Africa. Why, just yesterday, I kicked two naked people out of a garden!
jokes.setup: Tracy claims to be doing God's work in Africa
jokes.punchline: Reveals he's just recreating the Adam and Eve story
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Tracy logic - takes biblical reference and makes it literal/absurd. Perfect character voice and clever structure.

jokes.text: Simba, Rafiki! Click, click, click! Yeah, that's right, I just put you in your place in African.
jokes.setup: Tracy is supposedly in Africa speaking to locals
jokes.punchline: Uses Lion King character names and claims 'click, click, click' is speaking 'African'
jokes.explanation: Classic Tracy ignorance played perfectly - mixing Disney with stereotypes in character-appropriate way.

jokes.text: 'TGS with Tracy Jordan' without Tracy Jordan is an oxymoron, like 'liberal government' or 'female scientist.'
jokes.setup: Comparing show without Tracy to an oxymoron
jokes.punchline: Casually sexist examples delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Sharp character writing - Jack's casual elitism and sexism delivered with corporate efficiency.

jokes.text: Or Princeton football.
jokes.setup: Adding to Jack's list of oxymorons
jokes.punchline: Liz adds a sports dig that actually lands
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective addition to the bit - shows Liz can play Jack's game.

jokes.text: We were four and three in Ivy League play last year! Our quarterback, Henry Chang...
jokes.setup: Liz's Princeton football dig
jokes.punchline: Jack immediately gets defensive about his alma mater's athletics
jokes.explanation: Good character beat - Jack's pride wounded by something trivial, with specific details that make it funnier.

jokes.text: But you're looking out for us, right? Pounding on desks and doing whatever this is called?
jokes.setup: Liz asks if Jack is fighting for them
jokes.punchline: Jack is making some ridiculous hand gesture he can't name
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy that works in transcript - Jack doing corporate power moves he doesn't understand.

jokes.text: Power wagging
jokes.setup: Naming the gesture from previous joke
jokes.punchline: Made-up corporate term that sounds like dog behavior
jokes.explanation: Perfect 30 Rock wordplay - corporate speak meets animal behavior in two words. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Is it TNT? Are Rizzoli and Isles friends in real life?
jokes.setup: Jack mentions buying a network
jokes.punchline: Liz immediately asks about friendship status of TV cops
jokes.explanation: Perfect Liz priorities - ignores business implications to focus on celebrity gossip about procedural.

jokes.text: It's called TWINKS. / That's the name of your network? Isn't 'twink' a term for a young, hairless gay man?
jokes.setup: Jack proudly announces network name
jokes.punchline: Liz immediately identifies the sexual connotation Jack missed
jokes.explanation: Classic corporate obliviousness joke - Jack's cluelessness about obvious sexual terminology.

jokes.text: TWINKS is an acronym designed to project a positive gay image. 'TWINKS... Television With Individuals... Naive, Kinky, Shaved.' Okay.
jokes.setup: Jack tries to explain TWINKS as innocent acronym
jokes.punchline: Acronym somehow makes it worse with 'kinky' and 'shaved'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - Jack's attempt to fix the problem makes it exponentially worse. Perfect corporate backtracking.

jokes.text: When I was with D'Fwan on 'Queen of Jordan,' he spent $4,000 on chihuahua outfits for himself.
jokes.setup: Jack explaining the gay demographic's spending power
jokes.punchline: Specific absurd detail about dog clothes worn by humans
jokes.explanation: Good absurd detail that builds on established 30 Rock universe while making economic point.

jokes.text: 'I'm Afraid My Hands Are Tied'... is the only show anyone's watching on TWINKS.
jokes.setup: Jack uses common excuse phrase
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's literally a TV show title on his network
jokes.explanation: Exceptional wordplay - transforms cliché excuse into literal show title. Perfect economy and misdirection.

jokes.text: Sidney, it's Frank. 'TGS' is dead. Start booking me stand-up gigs. How many black women's colleges are there in the country?
jokes.setup: Frank immediately calls agent after hiatus news
jokes.punchline: Specific demographic targeting for his presumably offensive comedy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Frank character moment - immediately opportunistic with weirdly specific and inappropriate strategy.

jokes.text: What are you doing? / You just said we're going on a forced hiatus. I know what that means... time for Plan B. / Harriet Tubman School of Nursing? Yes and yes!
jokes.setup: Liz confused by Frank's phone call
jokes.punchline: Frank continuing his offensive targeting with historical figure's name
jokes.explanation: Continues Frank's character consistency - inappropriate but specific comedy venue targeting.

jokes.text: Good thing I kept my teaching certificate so I can still sub. Last time I taught, I was like Robin Williams in 'Dead Poets Society,' by which I mean I got fired.
jokes.setup: Toofer compares himself to inspirational teacher Robin Williams
jokes.punchline: Misdirection - only similarity is getting fired
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection joke - sets up heroic comparison then undercuts with mundane failure. Strong structure.

jokes.text: This is Asian JennaBaby. Excuse me, G.I., do you miss your girlfriend? I'm good at math. So it's not offensive.
jokes.setup: Jenna shows off her Asian-themed doll
jokes.punchline: Combines military and math stereotypes while claiming non-offensiveness
jokes.explanation: Classic Jenna obliviousness - piles on stereotypes while insisting she's not offensive.

jokes.text: I'm going back to the job I had in Holland... police psychic. A Dutch TV show is based on me.
jokes.setup: Lutz mentions going back to previous job
jokes.punchline: Reveals absurdly specific and unlikely profession with international fame
jokes.explanation: Great absurdist reveal - Lutz having exotic international career is perfectly unexpected.

jokes.text: At Harvard, we call them 'concentrations'...
jokes.setup: Liz asks what Toofer majored in
jokes.punchline: Toofer immediately corrects terminology to sound more elite
jokes.explanation: Perfect Toofer pretention - can't answer simple question without asserting Harvard superiority.

jokes.text: Let me just take out my idea journal. Hmm. This just says 'Bird Internet.'
jokes.setup: Kenneth promises to save the show with his ideas
jokes.punchline: His only idea is incomprehensible two-word phrase
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kenneth absurdism - builds expectation then delivers perfect nonsense. Highly quotable and memorable.

jokes.text: Like how Fredward helps iCarly. / No!
jokes.setup: Agent tries to explain his support role
jokes.punchline: Uses children's TV reference that horrifies Liz
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - incompetent agent using inappropriate pop culture analogy.

jokes.text: If you can get through the audition without pooping, it's yours.
jokes.setup: Simon offers Liz an Alpo commercial audition
jokes.punchline: Extremely low bar for success involves basic bodily control
jokes.explanation: Effective gross-out humor that emphasizes how far Liz's career has fallen.

jokes.text: Baby? Ah, yes, BABY... Black-Asian Bisexual Youths.
jokes.setup: Hank asks Jack about his baby
jokes.punchline: Jack thinks it's about demographics, creates awkward acronym
jokes.explanation: Sharp misdirection showing Jack's work obsession - can't hear 'baby' without thinking demographics.

jokes.text: My night nurse swears she calls me 'koskel,' which, in Trinidadian Creole, means 'stranger.'
jokes.setup: Jack mentions his baby's first word
jokes.punchline: Baby apparently calls him 'stranger' in another language
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy about absent parenting with specific cultural detail that makes it more believable and sad.

jokes.text: pardon my French... bonjour.
jokes.setup: Hank warns he's about to use strong language
jokes.punchline: Says 'hello' in French instead of swearing
jokes.explanation: Excellent subversion of 'pardon my French' cliché with literal interpretation. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Now, that's a joke, but I am really, really mad!
jokes.setup: Hank just made a pun
jokes.punchline: Explicitly explains his own joke while insisting on seriousness
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor - character acknowledging his own comedic moment while trying to maintain authority.

jokes.text: Remember when a movie was just a fella with a hat running away from a fella with no hair?
jokes.setup: Hank mentions not understanding Black Swan
jokes.punchline: Reduces all classic movies to simple chase formula
jokes.explanation: Clever observation that actually works for many classic films. Shows Hank's simple preferences perfectly.

jokes.text: Hank... there is a gay Jack Donaghy.
jokes.setup: Hank says there's no gay Jack to understand the network
jokes.punchline: Jack dramatically reveals such a person exists
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Devon's return with dramatic delivery. Sets up major plot point efficiently.

jokes.text: I sense something, a presence I've not felt since...
jokes.setup: Jack pausing dramatically
jokes.punchline: Obvious Star Wars reference about feeling Devon's presence
jokes.explanation: Standard pop culture reference but fits character rivalry dynamics well.

jokes.text: That would be like me hiring that mouse that keeps pooping in my slippers!
jokes.setup: Liz objects to hiring Jack's nemesis
jokes.punchline: Compares to her own specific gross apartment problem
jokes.explanation: Perfect Liz comparison - takes corporate metaphor and makes it about her gross living situation.

jokes.text: He's a gay shark, like the actor who played Jaws.
jokes.setup: Jack describes Devon as a 'gay shark'
jokes.punchline: Adds completely incorrect trivia about Jaws actor
jokes.explanation: Good Jack non-sequitur - combines valid business metaphor with completely wrong movie trivia.

jokes.text: When the administration started to falter because of our conspiracy... I mean, Obama's ineptitude
jokes.setup: Jack explaining why Devon fell from grace
jokes.punchline: Accidentally admits to conspiracy against Obama, quickly covers
jokes.explanation: Sharp political satire - Jack accidentally revealing corporate political machinations.

jokes.text: And we were all like, 'Whatever. We'll go to IHOP and not tell him!'
jokes.setup: Jack describing Devon's social exile from elite party
jokes.punchline: Billionaires acting like mean teenagers going to IHOP
jokes.explanation: Excellent contrast between elite setting and childish behavior. IHOP reference perfect for effect.

jokes.text: He's on LinkedIn, Lemon. He might as well be dead!
jokes.setup: Jack tracking down Devon's current status
jokes.punchline: Equates LinkedIn presence with career death
jokes.explanation: Brilliant corporate comedy - LinkedIn as career purgatory perfectly captures executive mindset.

jokes.text: What else crawls, Lemon? / Babies, Jack. You have one.
jokes.setup: Jack searching for crawling metaphor
jokes.punchline: Liz points out the obvious example he's forgetting
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Jack's absent parenting while advancing the metaphor setup.

jokes.text: would you buy a show about a girl television writer trying to have it all in the city and also she's a vampire, I guess?
jokes.setup: Jack asking for TV ideas
jokes.punchline: Liz describes her own life plus random vampire element
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-joke about the show itself, with vampire trend commentary. Perfect self-awareness.

jokes.text: I thought we understood that you are never to think that I understand anything!
jokes.setup: Jack angry about communication breakdown
jokes.punchline: Convoluted logic about misunderstanding being understood
jokes.explanation: Brilliant corporate double-talk that actually makes sense in its own twisted way.

jokes.text: Well, there have been a lot of 'Amazing Races' on since then, and I had to watch them and go online and comment on them!
jokes.setup: Jack asking why she has no plan B
jokes.punchline: Liz admits to wasting time on reality TV and online commenting
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Liz's procrastination through specific pop culture addiction.

jokes.text: I've been to L.A. Once. Olive just turns into Barham? Justice for Rodney King! How do I get to 10? It's the 10! Rodney King!
jokes.setup: Jack suggesting Liz move to LA
jokes.punchline: Liz's confused LA street navigation mixed with random social justice
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Liz's LA confusion through stream-of-consciousness navigation panic.

jokes.text: I'm Aaron Sorkin. 'The West Wing,' 'A Few Good Men,' 'The Social Network.' / 'Studio 60.' / Shut up.
jokes.setup: Sorkin listing his prestigious credits
jokes.punchline: Liz mentions his one failure, he immediately shuts her down
jokes.explanation: Sharp celebrity satire - even accomplished writers have sensitive spots about failures.

jokes.text: What is poking anyway? Why won't anybody do it to me? I'm cool!
jokes.setup: Sorkin complaining about social media
jokes.punchline: Reveals desperate need for Facebook validation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing successful writer's insecurity about digital age social validation.

jokes.text: We make horse buggies, and the first Model T just rolled into town. We're dinosaurs. / We don't need two metaphors.
jokes.setup: Sorkin uses elaborate metaphor about obsolescence
jokes.punchline: Liz critiques his writing technique
jokes.explanation: Excellent meta-commentary on overwrought writing style, perfectly delivered as writing criticism.

jokes.text: That's bad writing, not that it matters.
jokes.setup: Sorkin acknowledging the critique
jokes.punchline: Admits good writing doesn't matter anymore with defeated resignation
jokes.explanation: Sharp industry satire about quality becoming irrelevant in entertainment landscape.

jokes.text: It is not easy coming up with ideas to save the show, but this morning, I held up this sign in the 'Today Show' window!
jokes.setup: Kenneth claims to be working hard on saving the show
jokes.punchline: His grand strategy is holding up a sign on morning TV
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kenneth naivety - thinks morning show window appearance is sophisticated marketing strategy.

jokes.text: They sent light bulbs to save 'Friday Night Lights,' hot sauce to save 'Roswell,' and douche bags to save 'Entourage.'
jokes.setup: Frank explaining fan mail campaigns to save shows
jokes.punchline: Escalates from literal show references to insulting Entourage fans
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - builds pattern then delivers perfect insult that works on multiple levels.

jokes.text: Bird Internet!
jokes.setup: Kenneth needs idea for fan campaign
jokes.punchline: Returns to his incomprehensible journal entry
jokes.explanation: Strong callback that maintains the absurdist mystery. Still no explanation makes it funnier.

jokes.text: These aren't babies. They're organ farms!
jokes.setup: Jack arrives to find Devon with babies
jokes.punchline: Devon's paranoid explanation for baby triplets
jokes.explanation: Great Devon paranoia - takes innocent family situation and adds sinister conspiracy twist.

jokes.text: I married a shiny black dancer named Cashmere. We mixed our sperm together so no one would know whose baby is whose.
jokes.setup: Devon explaining his domestic situation
jokes.punchline: Bizarre fertility arrangement described matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Excellent absurdist family planning that feels perfectly Devon - overcomplicated and unnecessary.

jokes.text: You will be under me, and if there's one slipup, your ass is mine!
jokes.setup: Jack trying to establish dominance
jokes.punchline: Accidentally uses sexual language while trying to be authoritative
jokes.explanation: Good cringe humor - Jack falling into Devon's trap of sexual double entendres.

jokes.text: No! You are the spider. I am the sun! I dry up all the rain! / Yes! Freeing me, the spider, to climb up the spout again!
jokes.setup: Jack trying to control nursery rhyme metaphor
jokes.punchline: Devon turns it back around, both getting lost in the metaphor
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation of nursery rhyme metaphor warfare. Both characters trapped in their own cleverness.

jokes.text: But that's just a little shrapnel side effect from Vietnam.
jokes.setup: Hank mentions not being a straight shooter at urinals
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to war injury affecting bathroom habits
jokes.explanation: Good dark character detail - combines serious war trauma with mundane bathroom humor.

jokes.text: We throw him in a ditch!
jokes.setup: Jack saying Devon stops being useful
jokes.punchline: Hank enthusiastically suggests violent disposal
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Hank's casual attitude toward corporate violence.

jokes.text: I tried to call, but somebody spilled milky all over Daddy's phony. Didn't you, little Mykonos?
jokes.setup: Devon continuing excuse to corporate executives
jokes.punchline: Baby named after Greek party island
jokes.explanation: Good character detail - Devon would name baby after party destination. Fits his personality.

jokes.text: He threw up all over my jacket, and I just couldn't leave him, but... of course, I didn't want to miss this fabulous opportunity. I'm always just trying to balance work and family. It's kind of what I'm all about.
jokes.setup: Devon explaining bringing baby to meeting
jokes.punchline: Obvious manipulation presented as sincere family values
jokes.explanation: Good manipulation scene - Devon's fake sincerity about family-work balance to win over Hank.

jokes.text: This isn't even baby vomit on my jacket! It's mine! All mine!
jokes.setup: Devon's manipulation getting more elaborate
jokes.punchline: Reveals he vomited on himself for the manipulation
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation - Devon's dedication to manipulation includes self-degradation. Perfect character moment.

jokes.text: Just like 'Lean on Me,' in that a guy who looks like Morgan Freeman swung a bat at me.
jokes.setup: Lutz comparing substitute teaching to inspirational movie
jokes.punchline: Misdirection - only similarity is racial violence
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection - sets up heroic teaching comparison then delivers dark twist. Great structure.

jokes.text: unless you're married to it. So I had to marry each envelope and then divorce it
jokes.setup: Kenneth explaining why licking envelopes is a sin
jokes.punchline: Bizarre marital solution to envelope-licking moral dilemma
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kenneth logic - takes religious moral concern and creates absurd but internally consistent solution.

jokes.text: Kenneth, do you know what anthrax looks like?
jokes.setup: Kenneth describing sugar cubes being crushed in mail
jokes.punchline: Liz realizes Kenneth accidentally created terrorist threat
jokes.explanation: Excellent setup/payoff - Kenneth's innocent campaign accidentally creates bioterrorism scare. Perfect economy.

jokes.text: Thank you, Mentaalist. It is a gift and a curse.
jokes.setup: Sue helping catch the anthrax suspect
jokes.punchline: Her returning as the police psychic character
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to her earlier absurd career reveal

jokes.text: In the TV show, you bang me in a tulip field under credits.
jokes.setup: Lutz being arrested, mentions Dutch TV show about him
jokes.punchline: Reveals inappropriate romantic subplot in European procedural
jokes.explanation: Good callback that adds uncomfortable detail to Lutz's international fame story.

jokes.text: Mommy, what's that? / It's a toilet or a woman. It's whatever you need it to be.
jokes.setup: Child asking about abstract art
jokes.punchline: Parent gives nihilistic/sexist interpretation of modern art
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment about modern art interpretation with dark adult themes.

jokes.text: People of the sidewalk, we can't give up on the written word! We need stories. Because I don't have a Plan B!
jokes.setup: Liz making desperate street speech about importance of stories
jokes.punchline: Reveals it's actually about her personal career panic
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Liz's grandiose rhetoric hiding personal desperation.

jokes.text: I have a degree in Theater Tech with a minor in Movement!
jokes.setup: Liz continuing her desperate rant
jokes.punchline: Reveals she has completely useless academic credentials
jokes.explanation: Good self-deprecating humor about impractical education choices. Specific enough to be funny.

jokes.text: Better to ask who we used to be. Once, I was called Travel Agent. / I was an American Auto Worker. / And I played dynamite saxophone solos in rock-'n'-roll songs.
jokes.setup: Underground people with obsolete professions
jokes.punchline: Escalating from practical to increasingly specific/nostalgic jobs
jokes.explanation: Clever commentary on technological displacement with good escalation from practical to romantic obsolescence.

jokes.text: Come. We live under the subways with the CEO of Friendster.
jokes.setup: Underground community of obsolete professions
jokes.punchline: Includes failed social media CEO as fellow refugee
jokes.explanation: Great topical reference about tech failure. Friendster CEO as ultimate obsolete professional is perfect.

jokes.text: same cheap $300 tie as yesterday
jokes.setup: Devon cataloging Jack's disheveled state
jokes.punchline: $300 being considered 'cheap' for a tie
jokes.explanation: Good character detail about elite wealth perspective - $300 tie is 'cheap' to these characters.

jokes.text: I was going to take a picture where it looked like I was holding up the Leaning Tower of Pisa!
jokes.setup: Jack upset about losing Europe trip
jokes.punchline: Corporate executive excited about basic tourist photo cliché
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - sophisticated Jack excited about tourist trap photo opportunity.

jokes.text: Do you know what it's like to have triplets? It's just everything times three!
jokes.setup: Devon explaining family chaos
jokes.punchline: Reduces complex parenting to simple multiplication
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor about parenting multiples with Devon's analytical approach.

jokes.text: Three perfect little mouths that smile every time I walk through the door. Three tummies that just want their num-nums and zerberts.
jokes.setup: Devon's complaints about triplet chaos
jokes.punchline: Reveals genuine affection through baby talk
jokes.explanation: Excellent character development - Devon's manipulation facade drops to reveal genuine parental love.

jokes.text: Hey, I was bailed out of prison and told to come up here?
jokes.setup: Jack planned elaborate revenge scheme
jokes.punchline: Scheme apparently involved getting Frank arrested
jokes.explanation: Good absurdist moment suggesting Jack's elaborate revenge plots involve multiple criminal elements.

jokes.text: You're like Dora's friend. / Benny the Bull. / Benny the Bull.
jokes.setup: Devon comparing Jack's strength to children's character
jokes.punchline: Both executives know obscure Dora the Explorer reference
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing how parenting has affected both executives' cultural knowledge.

jokes.text: And I won't have to be the world's worst hooker! You want to party? It's $500 for kissing, $10,000 for snuggling, end of list.
jokes.setup: Kenneth celebrating not having to find new job
jokes.punchline: Reveals innocent pricing structure for prostitution
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Kenneth innocence - creates prostitution price list with completely innocent activities. Perfectly in character.

jokes.text: It's the snowiest winter we had in years in New York... in Africa.
jokes.setup: Tracy describing African weather
jokes.punchline: Accidentally reveals he's describing New York weather
jokes.explanation: Great setup for Tracy's reveal - his lie breaks down with geographic slip. Perfect Tracy logic.

jokes.text: Bird internet.
jokes.setup: Episode ending
jokes.punchline: Final callback to Kenneth's mysterious idea
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback ending - still no explanation of Bird Internet maintains the mystery and comedy.
